sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: A researcher compared glucose uptake in cultured epithelial cells at different extracellular glucose concentrations. Uptake increased rapidly at low glucose but approached a plateau at high concentrations. When a GLUT inhibitor was added, maximal uptake decreased. When cells were cooled to 4°C, uptake was also reduced, but the glucose concentration gradient was unchanged. The researcher concluded that most glucose entry occurred through a saturable carrier rather than simple diffusion.
questions.stem: What feature of Figure 1 best supports carrier-mediated transport?

choices.B: Uptake approaches a plateau at high glucose concentration
choices.A: Uptake decreases to zero at high glucose concentration
choices.C: Uptake is perfectly linear across all concentrations

explanations.B: Correct. Saturable transporters have a finite number of binding sites, so uptake rises and then approaches a maximum. That plateau supports carrier-mediated uptake.
explanations.A: The graph does not show uptake falling to zero.
explanations.C: A perfectly linear pattern would fit simple diffusion better.
explanations.D: The inhibitor lowers, rather than increases, uptake.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Glucose uptake rate as extracellular glucose concentration increases, with or without a GLUT inhibitor.

questions.stem: Why does the GLUT inhibitor decrease maximal uptake?

choices.D: It reduces the number or activity of functional glucose carriers
choices.B: It converts glucose into glycogen outside the cell

explanations.D: Correct. Blocking glucose carriers lowers the amount of transporter-mediated flux possible, reducing maximal uptake.
explanations.A: Increasing the gradient would tend to increase uptake, not lower Vmax.
explanations.B: Extracellular glycogen formation is not described.
explanations.C: Voltage-gated sodium channels do not transport glucose.

questions.stem: What type of transport is most likely represented by GLUT-mediated glucose entry down its concentration gradient?

choices.D: Simple diffusion through the lipid bilayer

explanations.A: Correct. GLUT proteins move glucose down its gradient without direct ATP hydrolysis, which is facilitated diffusion.
explanations.B: Primary active transport directly uses ATP, which is not described for GLUT.
explanations.C: Endocytosis involves vesicle formation, not carrier-mediated glucose flux.
explanations.D: Simple diffusion would not be saturable or inhibited by a carrier blocker.

questions.stem: Why does cooling cells reduce uptake even if the gradient is unchanged?

choices.C: Protein conformational cycling and membrane dynamics slow at low temperature

explanations.C: Correct. Carrier proteins depend on molecular motion and conformational changes; low temperature slows these processes and can reduce transport rate.
explanations.A: Glucose is not described as becoming charged.
explanations.B: The membrane does not disappear at 4°C.
explanations.D: The passage explicitly says the gradient was unchanged.

questions.stem: Which observation would best support simple diffusion instead of carrier transport?

choices.B: Uptake remains linear and nonsaturating across a wide concentration range
choices.C: A specific transporter inhibitor blocks uptake
choices.D: Transport rate depends on carrier expression

explanations.B: Correct. Simple diffusion is expected to scale roughly with the concentration gradient and not show transporter saturation.
explanations.A: A maximum is a hallmark of saturable carrier transport.
explanations.D: Dependence on carrier expression supports carrier-mediated uptake.

questions.passage: Students placed red blood cells into three solutions. In Solution A, cells maintained normal biconcave shape. In Solution B, cells swelled and some lysed. In Solution C, cells shrank and became crenated. The solutions contained different total concentrations of nonpenetrating solutes. The instructor emphasized that water moves across the membrane toward the compartment with higher effective osmolarity.
questions.stem: Which solution was hypotonic relative to the red blood cells?

explanations.A: Correct. Cells swell and may lyse in a hypotonic solution because water enters the cells. That is the pattern described for Solution B.
explanations.B: Solution A maintained normal shape, consistent with isotonic conditions.
explanations.C: Solution C caused shrinking, consistent with hypertonic conditions.
explanations.D: The observed cell shapes differ, so the solutions were not all isotonic.

questions.stem: What happened to cells in Solution C?

choices.D: Water left the cells, causing shrinkage
choices.A: Water entered the cells, causing lysis
choices.C: The membrane became freely permeable to hemoglobin

explanations.D: Correct. Crenation occurs when cells lose water in a hypertonic environment, causing them to shrink.
explanations.A: Water entry and lysis describe hypotonic conditions, not Solution C.
explanations.B: Osmosis occurred because cell volume changed.
explanations.C: The passage does not describe hemoglobin permeability.

questions.stem: Why are nonpenetrating solutes important for tonicity?

choices.C: They sustain osmotic gradients because they do not rapidly cross the membrane
choices.D: They make the membrane impermeable to all molecules

explanations.C: Correct. Nonpenetrating solutes remain effectively on one side of the membrane, so they influence net water movement and tonicity.
explanations.A: They drive, not eliminate, water movement when unequal.
explanations.B: They do not dissolve the membrane in this scenario.
explanations.D: They affect osmotic gradients but do not make the membrane impermeable to everything.

questions.stem: Which solution is most likely isotonic?

choices.D: A mixture of B and C only after lysis

explanations.B: Correct. Cells maintaining normal biconcave shape indicates no net water gain or loss, consistent with isotonic conditions.
explanations.A: Solution B caused swelling and lysis, indicating hypotonicity.
explanations.C: Solution C caused shrinkage, indicating hypertonicity.
explanations.D: The question asks which original solution is isotonic, and A fits that directly.

questions.stem: If urea rapidly penetrated the red blood cell membrane, why might its effect on tonicity be reduced?

choices.D: It would equilibrate across the membrane and fail to maintain an effective osmotic gradient
choices.A: It would become a nonpenetrating solute
choices.B: It would stop water from crossing aquaporins
choices.C: It would make the cytosol hypertonic forever

explanations.D: Correct. Solutes that cross rapidly do not maintain a persistent osmotic gradient, so they contribute less to effective tonicity.
explanations.A: Rapid penetration means it is not functioning as nonpenetrating.
explanations.B: Urea movement does not inherently block water channels.
explanations.C: Equilibration prevents a permanent hypertonic cytosol.
